title: Solar Crop Dryer
description: A low-cost solar-powered dryer that dramatically reduces post-harvest losses by 40%. Built using locally available materials, this dryer harnesses solar energy to dry fruits, vegetables, and grains efficiently — without any electricity costs. A single unit can dry 50 kg of produce per day, extending shelf life from 2 days to over 2 weeks.
materials: Wooden frame – 4m × 2m (locally available)
UV-resistant transparent plastic sheet (4m × 2.5m)
Galvanized wire mesh for drying rack (3m × 1.5m)
Black painted GI sheet for base (2m × 1.5m)
Hinges (4 nos.) and galvanized wire for ventilation flaps
Nails, screws, and wire staples
replicationSteps: Build a wooden frame 4 meters long, 2 meters wide, and 1 meter tall at the back tapering to 0.5 meters in front (sloped for solar angle).
Paint three interior sides and the base black using any heat-absorbing paint to maximize solar heat retention.
Stretch galvanized wire mesh horizontally inside the frame at two shelf levels to create drying surfaces.
Cover the entire top and front with UV-resistant transparent plastic sheeting, sealing all edges tightly with staples.
Drill 4-inch ventilation holes at the bottom front and top back — cover with mesh to keep insects out.
Place the dryer facing south (in India) for maximum sun exposure throughout the day.
Spread washed produce in a single layer on mesh shelves. Drying takes 4–6 hours on sunny days.

title: Rooftop Rainwater Harvesting
description: A simple rooftop rainwater harvesting system that collects and stores rainwater for use during dry spells. The system can store up to 5,000 liters from a single monsoon and has helped irrigate half an acre during drought months, reducing dependence on borewells by 60%.
materials: Corrugated GI roofing sheets (existing roof)
PVC gutters – 3-inch diameter (10 meters total)
PVC pipes – 3-inch and 2-inch (8 meters total)
First-flush diverter (locally fabricated or purchased)
Storage tank – 5,000-liter capacity (HDPE)
Gravel and sand filter (2 kg each)
PVC fittings – T-joints, elbows, end caps
replicationSteps: Install PVC gutters along the lower edge of all roof sections to catch rainwater runoff.
Connect downpipes from gutters toward a central collection point.
Install a first-flush diverter — this discards the first 20 liters of each rain event (which carries dust and bird droppings).
Add a gravel-sand filter before the tank inlet to remove debris.
Connect the filtered pipe to the HDPE storage tank via a float valve to prevent overflow.
Install a tap at 10 cm from the bottom of the tank (sediment settles below the tap).
Cover the tank with a fitted lid and paint it white or silver to reduce water temperature.

title: Low Cost Drip Irrigation
description: A gravity-fed drip irrigation system constructed from PVC pipes and locally available components. Compared to flood irrigation, this system reduces water usage by 60% and fertilizer consumption by 30%, while increasing yields by up to 25%. Designed for plots of 0.25 to 1 acre with a total setup cost under ₹3,000.
materials: HDPE elevated water tank – 500 liters
Steel stand for tank elevation – 2 meters height
PVC main pipe – 1-inch diameter (20 meters)
PVC sub-main pipes – ½-inch diameter (50 meters total)
Inline drip emitters – 2 LPH flow rate (100 nos.)
Filter screen at tank outlet
PVC fittings – T-joints, end caps, elbows
replicationSteps: Elevate a 500-liter HDPE tank on a 2-meter steel stand to generate 0.2 bar gravity pressure — enough for drip emitters.
Install a 100-mesh filter screen at the tank outlet to prevent emitter clogging.
Lay 1-inch PVC main pipe along the length of the field; cap the far end.
Connect ½-inch sub-main pipes at 60 cm intervals, running perpendicular to the main pipe toward crop rows.
Punch holes and insert inline drip emitters every 30 cm along each sub-main pipe.
Fill the tank with water and check each emitter for flow. Replace any blocked emitters.
Run for 45 minutes twice daily (morning and evening). Adjust frequency with seasons.

title: Manual Multi-Purpose Farming Tool
description: A single hand tool that performs five different farming operations: weeding, hoeing, furrowing, earthing-up, and inter-row cultivation. Designed with a universal quick-swap connector, the attachments change in under 60 seconds with no tools required. This single tool replaces five, cutting equipment costs by ₹2,000+ per farmer.
materials: Mild steel flat bar – 25mm × 6mm (3 meters total)
Steel pipe for handle – 25mm outer diameter, 1.2 m length
Universal bayonet connector (locally fabricated)
M8 bolts and nuts (4 sets)
Anti-rust black paint (1 small tin)
Hardwood grip for handle end (optional)
replicationSteps: Cut mild steel flat bar into 5 attachment blanks of different dimensions using an angle grinder.
Forge and shape each blade at a local blacksmith: narrow weeder (V-shape), flat hoe, furrower (pointed), earthing-up plate (angled), and inter-row cultivator (rake).
Drill two 8mm bolt holes in the socket end of each attachment.
Fabricate a universal socket on one end of the handle pipe — two cross-drilled holes that accept the bolt pattern.
Each attachment slides into the socket and is secured with two wing nuts. Swap time: under 60 seconds.
Grind all edges to working sharpness and spray all surfaces with anti-rust paint.
Test all 5 field operations on a small plot before daily use.

title: Bicycle Powered Water Pump
description: Converts a standard bicycle into a human-powered water pump without any permanent modifications. The system can pump water from 6 meters depth at 500 liters per hour — enough for 0.25 acres of vegetables daily. Zero fuel cost, zero electricity, and completely reversible — the bicycle returns to normal riding in 10 minutes.
materials: Old bicycle – any type with rear wheel accessible
Centrifugal pump head – 1-inch ports, 0.5 HP equivalent
V-belt (B-type, 1.2m length) and two V-pulleys
Mild steel stand frame (40mm angle iron, welded)
Suction pipe – 1-inch flexible PVC (8 meter length)
Delivery pipe – 1-inch PVC (field length as needed)
Jubilee clamps (4 nos.) and pipe fittings
replicationSteps: Weld an A-frame steel stand that lifts the bicycle's rear wheel 10 cm off the ground while keeping the frame stable.
Mount a V-belt pulley (15cm diameter) onto the rear axle of the bicycle using an adapter collar.
Attach a smaller pulley (8cm diameter) to the centrifugal pump shaft to create a 2:1 speed ratio.
Connect both pulleys with a B-type V-belt. Adjust tension by sliding the pump mount.
Connect the suction pipe to the pump inlet, lowering it into the water source to the desired depth.
Connect the delivery pipe to the pump outlet and route it to the field.
Pedal at comfortable cycling speed to pump 500 liters per hour. One person can irrigate a quarter-acre plot in 2 hours.

title: Portable Grain Cleaning Machine
description: A hand-operated grain cleaning machine that separates grain from chaff, dust, and foreign particles using a rope-driven fan mechanism. Processes 100 kg of grain per hour with zero electricity. The portable design allows it to be shared between multiple farmers. Reduces post-harvest grain losses from 8% to under 2%.
materials: Hardwood box frame – 1m × 0.6m × 1.2m (sal or teak)
Steel fan blades – 4-blade, 35cm diameter
Rope-pull flywheel – 40cm diameter with 1.5m cord
Wire mesh screens – 2 sizes: 6mm and 3mm aperture
Sheet metal hopper – 40cm × 30cm opening
Collection trays – 2 pieces (galvanized iron)
Ball bearings for fan shaft (2 nos.)
replicationSteps: Build the outer hardwood box frame with the hopper opening centered at the top and two angled output chutes at the bottom.
Mount the two wire-mesh screens inside the box at a 15-degree angle, one above the other (coarse screen on top).
Install the fan assembly with steel blades in the middle chamber, mounted on a shaft with ball bearings for smooth rotation.
Attach the rope-pull flywheel to the fan shaft extending outside the box for the operator to pull.
Create two exit paths: clean grain falls through both screens and exits via the grain chute; chaff is blown out via the side vent.
Test with a small batch of grain — pull the rope to spin the fan, then pour grain into the hopper slowly.
One person pulls the rope rhythmically while another feeds grain. Output: 100 kg of clean grain per hour.

title: Vermicompost Bio-Fertilizer Unit
description: A low-cost vermicomposting bed built from locally available bricks and gunny sacks that converts kitchen waste, cow dung, and crop residue into rich organic fertilizer. Produces 50 kg of vermicompost every 45 days from a single 6×3 ft bed. Eliminates the need for chemical fertilizers, cutting input costs by ₹4,000 per acre per season while improving soil structure and water retention.
materials: Bricks – 200 nos. (for 6×3 ft bed walls, 1 ft height)
Gunny sacks or shade net – 2 pieces (for covering)
Eisenia fetida earthworms – 1 kg starter culture
Cow dung – 50 kg (fresh, as bedding base)
Crop residue, dry leaves, kitchen vegetable waste
Water (for moisture maintenance)
Neem leaves – 2 kg (natural pest deterrent)
replicationSteps: Construct a 6×3 ft brick bed, 1 foot high, with no cemented base to allow drainage and earthworm movement.
Add a 4-inch layer of moist cow dung as the base bedding and let it pre-compost for 3 days.
Introduce 1 kg of Eisenia fetida earthworms (available from agriculture universities or online) into the bed.
Add a 2-inch layer of chopped crop residue and kitchen vegetable waste on top. Avoid oily, salty, or cooked food.
Keep the bed moist (not wet) — sprinkle water daily and cover with gunny sack to maintain 60% moisture.
Continue adding organic waste in thin layers every 3-4 days. The worms will multiply and process it.
Harvest rich vermicompost after 40-45 days by pushing the material to one side — the worms migrate toward fresh food, making separation easy.

title: Low-Cost Poly Tunnel Greenhouse
description: A simple bamboo-framed greenhouse covered with UV-stabilized polythene film that extends the growing season by 3 months and protects crops from unseasonal rain, frost, and pests. A 10×4 metre tunnel costs under ₹3,500, yields 3× more vegetables than open-field cultivation, and can be dismantled and moved between seasons.
materials: Bamboo poles – 2-inch diameter, 4-metre length (20 nos.)
UV-stabilized polythene film – 200 micron, 12m × 6m roll
GI wire – 16-gauge (50 metres) for binding and tensioning
Wooden stakes – 2-ft length (20 nos.) for anchoring
Nylon rope – 50 metres
Clips or clamps – 30 nos. for film attachment
replicationSteps: Mark out an area 10 metres long × 4 metres wide. Drive wooden stakes every 1 metre along both long sides, 30 cm deep.
Bend one 4-metre bamboo pole into an arch from stake to stake — the natural flexibility forms a dome 2 metres high at centre.
Lash each arch firmly with GI wire at the base stakes. Space arches every 1 metre for 10 hoops total.
Run 3 horizontal bamboo lines along the full tunnel length (top and two sides) tying them to all arches for rigidity.
Drape the UV polythene film over the frame from one end to the other, pulling it taut and clipping at each arch.
Bury the film edges 15 cm into the soil on both sides to seal the tunnel against wind and insects.
Leave both tunnel ends open (or fit removable flaps) for ventilation. Install drip lines inside before sealing.

title: Community Seed Bank System
description: A village-level seed bank system using upcycled glass jars, neem leaf powder, and a coded register to preserve and share 40+ traditional crop varieties. The seed bank has successfully maintained 12 drought-resistant varieties that were on the verge of extinction in the region. Each participating family deposits seeds after harvest and borrows improved seeds before sowing.
materials: Glass jars with tight lids – 1 litre capacity (50 nos.)
Neem leaf powder – 500 grams (natural pest repellent)
Silica gel packets – 1 per jar (moisture absorber)
Label paper and waterproof marker
Wooden rack or almirah (for cool, dark storage)
Register notebook (for seed inventory and lending)
replicationSteps: Collect healthy, mature seeds from the best plants in the field (not hybrid seeds — only open-pollinated varieties).
Dry seeds in shade for 5-7 days until moisture content is below 10% — test by bending: a dry seed snaps, a wet seed bends.
Mix 1 tablespoon of neem powder per kg of seeds as a natural pesticide before storage.
Fill clean, dry glass jars with seeds and add one silica gel packet per jar to absorb moisture.
Label each jar with crop name, variety name, village source, harvest year, and germination rate.
Store jars in a cool, dark, dry location (below 20°C) — avoid temperature fluctuations.
Maintain a register: each family deposits surplus seeds after harvest and may borrow seeds before sowing, returning 1.5× quantity at next harvest.

title: Backyard Mushroom Cultivation Unit
description: A low-investment mushroom cultivation system using rice straw and recycled HDPE bags in a small 8×10 ft shaded space. Produces 8-10 kg of fresh oyster mushrooms per 25-day cycle with a total cost of under ₹800 per batch. Mushrooms sell at ₹120-180 per kg in local markets, generating ₹700-1,200 net profit per cycle — a reliable income source during off-seasons.
materials: Paddy straw – 20 kg (dried and chopped to 6-inch pieces)
Oyster mushroom spawn – 500 grams (Pleurotus ostreatus)
HDPE bags – 15×24 inch, 200 gauge (20 nos.)
Calcium carbonate (lime) – 100 grams (for pasteurisation)
Bamboo or iron shelf (in a shaded, humid room)
Sprayer bottle for daily misting with clean water
replicationSteps: Chop paddy straw into 6-inch pieces and soak overnight in water mixed with 1% lime solution (10 grams lime per litre).
Drain thoroughly and squeeze out excess water — the straw should feel damp but not dripping.
Fill HDPE bags in alternate layers: 3-inch straw layer, then a thin spawn layer, repeated 4-5 times. Tie bag top loosely.
Punch 8-10 small holes (5mm) along the sides of each bag using a sharp nail — these will become mushroom pinning sites.
Hang bags vertically or stack on bamboo shelves in a shaded, humid room. Maintain 25-30°C temperature.
Mist bags with clean water twice daily to maintain 80-85% relative humidity. Avoid direct sunlight.
White mycelium appears in 7-10 days. Small pins emerge at holes by day 12-14. Harvest full clusters when caps reach 5-8 cm — twist off at base.

title: Neem-Based Organic Pesticide
description: A highly effective organic pesticide prepared from neem seeds, garlic, and chilli that controls over 200 insect pest species without harming beneficial insects, pollinators, or soil microbes. Costs less than ₹50 per 10-litre batch versus ₹350 for equivalent chemical pesticides. When used consistently, reduces pesticide expenditure by ₹6,000-8,000 per acre per season.
materials: Neem seeds (dried) – 500 grams
Garlic – 100 grams
Green chilli – 100 grams
Water – 10 litres
Cloth for filtering
Soap (any detergent) – 5 ml as emulsifier
Plastic container with lid – 15-litre capacity
replicationSteps: Crush 500 grams of dried neem seeds coarsely using a stone or hammer. Soak overnight in 2 litres of water.
Grind 100 grams garlic and 100 grams green chilli into a fine paste with a small amount of water.
Combine the neem water (with seeds), garlic paste, and chilli paste in a 15-litre container. Add remaining 8 litres of water.
Stir vigorously and let the mixture ferment in a covered container for 10-12 hours in shade.
Filter through a cloth to remove all solid particles. Add 5 ml of soap (acts as a sticking agent on leaves).
Dilute the concentrate 1:10 with water before use (1 litre of concentrate + 9 litres water per spray).
Spray on both sides of leaves early morning or evening. Repeat every 5-7 days. Effective against aphids, whitefly, leaf miners, and borers.

title: Rope Pump Well Water Lifter
description: A simple rope-and-washer pump that lifts water from open wells up to 8 metres depth using only a bicycle wheel and human power. The rope carries a chain of rubber disc washers through a PVC pipe, pushing water upward continuously. Costs ₹900 to build entirely from recycled materials and can deliver 1,500 litres per hour — enough for household needs and a small kitchen garden.
materials: Old bicycle wheel – 1 nos. (26-inch preferred)
PVC pipe – 1.5-inch diameter, 10 metres length
Nylon rope – 12mm diameter, 25 metres (forms the endless loop)
Rubber discs (washers) – cut from old tyre inner tube, 20 nos.
Wooden frame – for mounting the wheel above the well
Short length of GI pipe for outlet spout
replicationSteps: Cut 20 rubber discs (washers) from an old bicycle inner tube, sized to fit snugly inside the PVC pipe.
Thread nylon rope through each rubber washer and tie/knot firmly every 50 cm along the rope's length.
Lower the PVC pipe vertically into the well, submerging the lower end 1 metre below the minimum water level.
Feed the rope-with-washers through the PVC pipe and bring it over the bicycle wheel mounted above the well.
Connect the rope into an endless loop. The wheel rotation pulls washers upward through the pipe, pushing water ahead of each washer.
Build a simple wooden frame to hold the bicycle wheel 30 cm above the PVC pipe opening.
Turn the wheel by hand or attach a handle. The water exits at the top outlet into a bucket or trough.

title: Bamboo Micro-Irrigation Channels
description: A traditional-meets-modern micro-irrigation system using split bamboo culms as gravity-flow channels to deliver water precisely to crop root zones. Covers 0.5 acres at a cost of just ₹1,100 — 70% cheaper than PVC pipe systems. Bamboo is completely biodegradable and can be harvested from farm boundaries, making the system essentially free after the first year when bamboo regrows.
materials: Mature bamboo culms – 4-inch diameter, 3-metre sections (30 nos.)
Bamboo saw or metal saw for splitting
Wooden pegs – 50 nos. (for elevating channels at proper gradient)
Clay or natural rubber sealant (for joints)
Small stones / gravel (for drip points at root zones)
Simple sluice gate (thin plywood + rubber gasket, for flow control)
replicationSteps: Harvest mature bamboo (3+ years old) from farm periphery. Cut into 3-metre sections.
Split each culm lengthwise using a sharp saw or machete to create U-shaped half-pipe channels.
Remove internal nodes with a rod so water flows freely through the full length.
Map the field: design a herringbone channel layout — one main channel feeding lateral channels every 1.5 metres.
Elevate the main bamboo channel on 30-cm wooden pegs at a 1:100 gradient (1 cm drop per metre) to ensure gravity flow.
Connect lateral channels at junctions sealed with clay. Place small stones at the end of each lateral as a diffuser to prevent soil erosion at delivery points.
Open the sluice gate to the water source — water flows through the bamboo network and drips gently at root zones.

title: Solar Biogas Digester
description: A compact fixed-dome biogas plant (1 cubic metre capacity) combining cattle dung fermentation with a solar-heated digester jacket that accelerates gas production by 40% even in winter months. Produces 1.5 hours of cooking gas daily from 5 kg of cow dung. Replaces LPG cylinders entirely for a family of 5, saving ₹900 per month on fuel, while the spent slurry is the best liquid fertilizer available.
materials: Bricks – 500 nos. + cement + sand (for dome construction)
GI pipe – 1-inch diameter (for gas outlet, 3 metre length)
PVC flexible gas pipe – 6mm (5 metres, to connect to stove)
Biogas stove burner – 1 nos.
Black polythene sheet – for solar heating jacket wrap
Inlet and outlet slurry pipes – PVC 4-inch diameter
Iron gate valve – 1-inch (for gas flow control)
replicationSteps: Dig a 1.5-metre diameter, 1.5-metre deep pit. Construct a fixed dome using bricks and cement — hemispherical shape with a 1-metre dome height above ground level.
Build a separate mixing tank at the inlet to combine equal parts fresh cow dung and water before feeding.
Plaster the interior of the dome with two coats of cement and one coat of bitumen paint to make it gas-tight.
Install inlet pipe (for slurry entry), gas outlet pipe at the dome apex, and overflow slurry outlet.
Wrap the above-ground portion of the dome with black polythene as a solar heating jacket — this raises digester temperature by 5-8°C in winter, maintaining fermentation.
Charge with 200 litres of cow dung slurry (1:1 dung:water) as starter. Gas production begins in 10-15 days.
Feed 5 kg of fresh dung mixed with 5 litres of water daily. Collect gas through the pipeline to the kitchen stove.

title: Hand-Operated Groundnut Oil Expeller
description: A wooden screw-press oil expeller built by a local carpenter that processes 10 kg of groundnuts per hour into cold-pressed oil with zero electricity. Extracts 40-42% oil from groundnuts versus 35% from chemical expellers, producing premium cold-pressed oil that sells at ₹250/litre compared to ₹150 for refined oil. The press cake (residue) is sold as protein-rich animal feed, generating 100% income from every kilogram processed.
materials: Dense hardwood block – 30cm × 30cm × 60cm (for press barrel)
Steel screw – 5-cm diameter, 50-cm length with handle (forged)
Steel plate – 5mm thick (for pressing disc)
Steel perforated plate (30% open area) – for oil drainage
Collection tray – GI sheet, 40cm × 40cm
Stainless steel muslin cloth – for filtering extracted oil
Wooden base frame – for stabilising press during operation
replicationSteps: Have a carpenter bore a 15-cm diameter cylindrical cavity through the hardwood block vertically — this is the press barrel.
Line the lower half of the cavity with the perforated steel plate so oil can drain out while seeds remain pressed.
A local blacksmith forges the 5-cm steel screw with a 50-cm cross-handle for leverage. The screw threads into a steel nut fixed at the top of the barrel.
Load 1 kg of dried, shelled groundnuts into the barrel. Turn the screw handle clockwise, applying gradual pressure.
Oil flows out through the perforations and drains into the collection tray in 8-10 minutes per batch.
Filter the collected oil through stainless muslin cloth twice to remove fine particles. Bottle the clear golden oil immediately.
Remove the pressed cake from the barrel — it is 30-32% protein and sold as livestock feed at ₹25-30 per kg.
